en.extract:
The Angola greenbul (Phyllastrephus viridiceps) is a species of passerine bird in the bulbul family, Pycnonotidae. It is found in northwest Angola.  Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests and subtropical or tropical moist montane forests. It was formerly considered to be a subspecies of the white-throated greenbul.


Taxonomy
The Angola greenbul was formally described in 1955 by the Canadian zoologist Austin L. Rand based on specimens collected near the town of Cambeta in northern Angola. He considered it to be a subspecies of the white-throated greenbul and coined the trinomial name Phyllastrephus albigularis viridiceps. It is now considered as a separate species based on differences in vocalization and morphology. The genus name combines Ancient Greek φυλλον/phullon  meaning "leaf" with στρεφω/strephō meaning "to toss" or "to turn". The specific epithet viridiceps combines Latin viridis meaning "green" with -ceps meaning "-crowned".

The '''Angola greenbul''' ('''''Phyllastrephus viridiceps''''') is a species of [[passerine]] bird in the [[bulbul]] family, [[Pycnonotidae]]. It is found in northwest Angola.  Its natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s. It was formerly considered to be a [[subspecies]] of the [[white-throated greenbul]].

==Taxonomy==
The Angola greenbul was [[species description|formally described]] in 1955 by the Canadian zoologist [[Austin L. Rand]] based on specimens collected near the town of Cambeta in northern Angola. He considered it to be a [[subspecies]] of the [[white-throated greenbul]] and coined the [[trinomial name]] ''Phyllastrephus albigularis viridiceps''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Rand | first=Austin L. | author-link=Austin L. Rand | date=1955 | title=Tree new bulbuls from Africa (class Aves) | journal=Fieldiana. Zoology | volume=34 | issue=33 | pages=333-336 [336] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2777092 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1960 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=9 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=269 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480944 }}</ref> It is now considered as a separate species based on differences in vocalization and morphology.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Bulbuls | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/bulbuls/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=6 September 2024}}</ref> The genus name combines [[Ancient Greek]] φυλλον/''phullon '' meaning "leaf" with στρεφω/''strephō'' meaning "to toss" or "to turn".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=Phyllastrephus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=Phyllastrephus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=6 September 2024 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''viridiceps'' combines [[Latin]] ''viridis'' meaning "green" with ''-ceps'' meaning "-crowned".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=viridiceps | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=viridiceps | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=6 September 2024 }}</ref>
